title: Accelerating quantum Gibbs sampling without quantum walks
authors: Jiaqi Leng, Jiaqing Jiang, Lin Lin
abstract: Szegedy's quantum walk gives a generic quadratic speedup for reversible classical Markov chains, but extending this mechanism to quantum Gibbs sampling has remained challenging beyond special cases. We present a walk-free quantum algorithm for preparing purified Gibbs states with a quadratic improvement in spectral-gap dependence for a broad class of quantum Gibbs samplers that satisfy exact Kubo-Martin-Schwinger detailed balance. Our main structural result is an explicit factorization of the corresponding parent Hamiltonian into noncommutative first-order operators. This turns purified Gibbs-state preparation into a singular-value filtering problem and enables a quantum singular value transformation algorithm with quadratically improved gap dependence under standard coherent-access assumptions. The framework applies to several efficiently implementable Gibbs samplers beyond the Davies setting. We also introduce an auxiliary dissipative dynamics based on the same factorization, which can be used to generate warm starts in the doubled Hilbert space in metastable regimes.
summary: This paper introduces a new quantum algorithm for preparing thermal (Gibbs) states that is faster than previous quantum walk-based methods. By using a mathematical factorization of the system's Hamiltonian, the authors reduce the computational complexity related to the spectral gap from 1/Delta to 1/sqrt(Delta). They also provide a method to generate good starting states for the algorithm using auxiliary dissipative dynamics. This advancement makes the simulation of many-body systems at finite temperatures more efficient on future quantum hardware.
main_problem: The challenge of efficiently preparing purified Gibbs states on a quantum computer, specifically overcoming the quadratic dependence on the spectral gap found in standard Szegedy-type quantum walk approaches.
main_result: The authors present a walk-free quantum algorithm that achieves a quadratic improvement in spectral gap dependence, reducing it from O(1/Delta) to O(1/sqrt(Delta)), and introduce a warm-start strategy using auxiliary dissipative dynamics.
method: The algorithm uses a singular-value filtering approach via Quantum Singular Value Transformation (QSVT) applied to an explicit factorization of the parent Hamiltonian into non-commutative first-order operators.
model_or_system: Quantum many-body systems at finite temperature governed by Lindbladian dynamics that satisfy the Kubo-Martin-Schwinger (KMS) detailed balance condition, including Davies and CKG samplers.
key_observables: Thermal expectation values of observables, spectral gap (Delta), and overlap between the prepared state and the target purified Gibbs state.
important_parameters: Inverse temperature (beta), spectral gap (Delta), precision (epsilon), and the number of jump operators (J).
main_assumptions: The Lindbladian must satisfy the KMS detailed balance condition and require coherent access to the first-order factors and a warm-start initial state.
figures_summary: Numerical simulations of the Transverse-Field Ising Model (TFIM) showing the overlap of auxiliary dynamics with the target state as a function of time and temperature, and a comparison of spectral gaps for different Lindbladian classes.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of Gibbs sampling speedups, presents a mathematical factorization of the parent Hamiltonian, develops the QSVT-based algorithm, proposes an auxiliary dynamics for warm-starting, and validates the approach with numerical simulations on the TFIM.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and many-body dynamics as it provides a more efficient way to simulate thermal equilibrium states and addresses the practical difficulty of state preparation in the low-temperature regime.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Circuit schematics for the block-encoding of the discretized first-order operator B. Panels (a) and (b) show the dressed block-encodings of ˜UB♯and ˜U(B♭)⊺. Panel (c) shows the full circuit assembling these ingredients with the quadrature-weight preparation oracle Uprep and a branch ancilla to realize the linear combination corresponding to I ⊗B♯ j,tk −(B♭ j,tk)⊺⊗I.

Figure 2. Numerical simulation of the auxiliary dissipative dynamics with “unitary dressing” for a 3-qubit transverse- field Ising model. We plot the overlap Tr[ρ(t)∣σ1/2⟩⟩⟨⟨σ1/2∣] as a function of the evolution time t, for several inverse tem- peratures β. As β increases, the global mixing time grows substantially, but the overlap with the target purified Gibbs state already exceeds 0.2 at t = 1 for all values of β shown. Therefore, the auxiliary dynamics can generate states with non-negligible target overlap on a timescale much shorter than the global mixing time.
Figure 3. The ratio between the spectral gap of the new Hamiltonian H0 and the parent Hamiltonian H.
Figure 4. Spectral gaps of three classes of Lindbladian operators: the auxiliary dynamics (without unitary dressing), the auxiliary dynamics with unitary dressing, and the original DLL Gibbs sampler.
